mappings.rationaleAI: Engineering's history, scope, and relationship with science and technology address general conceptions of technology (A). Coverage of infrastructure, water systems, and climate change adaptation relates to technology's effects on social life and environment (B).

British industrialization covering steam power, textiles, and iron production exemplifies the Industrial Revolution period (C.3) with advances in power technology (C.3.a) and development of industries (C.3.b). Economic transformation through technological change demonstrates technology and wealth (B.1).

Innovation as a process driving economic and social development through technological change relates to general conceptions of technology (A) and technology and wealth (B.1). Coverage of disruptive technologies including AI and their implications connects to 20th-century technology developments (C.4).
The co-evolution of weapons systems, military tactics, and technological capabilities from prehistoric times to autonomous warfare directly addresses technology and war (B.2).
Education systems examined through historical and social perspectives including adapting pedagogy for an increasingly digital world directly addresses technology and education (B.3).

Comparative analysis of why some countries achieved prosperity covering technological change and institutional frameworks addresses technology and wealth (B.1) and the export of Western technology to underdeveloped regions (B.6).
Climate change science covering global warming, ecological disruption, and solutions to decarbonization directly addresses the effects of technology on the environment (B.7).

Humanity's permanent impact on Earth's geological record through pollution, species invasions, mass extinction, and fossil fuel combustion directly addresses effects of technology on the environment (B.7).

Earliest Australopithecines through the invention of agriculture and development of cities covers the beginnings of technology including early communities, stone tools, and agricultural origins (C.1.a).
Ancient Greek civilization covering political institutions and material culture connects to technological achievements of Greece (C.1.c).
Medieval European history covering agriculture, trade, and the emergence of commerce networks relates to medieval technological advances (C.2.a).

Sixteenth and seventeenth century developments covering invention of scientific instruments, new investigative methods, and technologies enabling discovery relate to the emergence of Western technology 1500-1750 (C.2.b).

History of computing from ancient counting aids through the semiconductor revolution traces the development of electronic digital computing and the information age, central to Space Age technology (C.4.b).
Development of nuclear fission and fusion technologies for energy production directly addresses nuclear power as a Space Age technology (C.4.b).
Solar photovoltaics, wind power, hydropower, battery storage, and grid management represent advances in energy technology within 20th-century and contemporary technological development (C.4.b).
Engineering analysis of energy conversion and distribution systems including fossil fuels, renewables, and nuclear power addresses advances in energy technology (C.4.b).
Design, construction, and deployment of autonomous machines for manufacturing, hospitals, and space exploration represents automation technology in the modern era (C.4.b).
AI history, achievements, and future directions including practical applications in everyday life relate to automation and electronic digital computing as modern technology (C.4.b).

Sustainability addressing human impacts on planetary systems, material and energy flows, and technological interventions for sustainable societies relates to the effects of technology on the environment (B.7).
Civil engineering covering bridges, tunnels, and urbanization addresses general conceptions of technology (A) and the role of engineering across the history of civilization (C).
